concise:
Ayah 1–34 · linked video: 01. Al-Qamar (Ayah 1-34)

Surah Al-Qamar: Commentary on Ayahs 1-34

Ayah 1

ٱقْتَرَبَتِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ وَٱنشَقَّ ٱلْقَمَرُ

The Hour has drawn near, and the moon has split.

This ayah opens with a reference to one of the most remarkable miracles witnessed during the life of the Prophet ﷺ. The splitting of the moon occurred when the Quraysh demanded a sign from the Messenger ﷺ. By Allah’s ﷻ permission, he pointed at the moon and it split into two distinct parts before their very eyes, then rejoined.

An important question arises: did anyone else in the world witness this event, or was it shown exclusively to the Arabs? It’s entirely possible that Allah ﷻ made this miracle visible only to the people of Arabia, as He shows what He wills to whom He wills. This was a specific sign for those who were demanding proof from the Prophet ﷺ.

However, historical accounts suggest that this phenomenon may have been witnessed elsewhere. There are reports of a king in India, along the Malabar Coast, who observed this event. When he later encountered the Companions (RA), he embraced Islam because what he had witnessed years earlier was finally explained to him. This remains an area worthy of further research for those interested in Islamic history.

The ayah begins with اقْتَرَبَتِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ – “the Hour has approached” – placing the splitting of the moon in the context of the Day of Judgment drawing near. The pairing of these two events is profound: a cosmic sign in the sky serves as a reminder of the ultimate cosmic event to come.

Ayah 2

وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ ءَايَةً يُعْرِضُوا۟ وَيَقُولُوا۟ سِحْرٌ مُّسْتَمِرٌّ

And if they see a sign, they turn away and say, “This is continuous magic.”

Despite witnessing such an extraordinary miracle, Allah ﷻ reveals the reality of their hearts: no matter what sign they were shown, they would dismiss it. The phrase وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ ءَايَةً – “and if they see a miracle” – uses the conditional, indicating that this is their pattern of behavior. It wasn’t just about the moon splitting; any miracle would have been met with the same rejection.

Their response was to cry out سِحْرٌ مُّسْتَمِرٌّ – “continuous magic.” The word mustamirr conveys something that doesn’t stop, that keeps going. In their desperation to deny the truth, they claimed this was magic that just kept happening, as if the Prophet ﷺ had some endless supply of magical tricks.

This ayah exposes a profound truth: the problem wasn’t a lack of evidence. The problem was in their hearts. They had been asking for signs, demanding proof, but when it came, they simply moved the goalposts. This is a warning for all of us about the danger of closed hearts that refuse to see truth even when it’s presented clearly.

Ayah 3

وَكَذَّبُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۚ وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ

And they denied and followed their desires, while every matter has a time of settlement.

Allah ﷻ identifies the root cause of their rejection: وَكَذَّبُوا۟ – they lied against the truth, and وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ – they followed their vain desires. This is what they’ve always done. The truth was never really the issue; their attachment to their own whims and desires was what prevented them from accepting it.

But then comes a powerful reminder: وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ – every matter will eventually come to rest, will be settled. This is Allah’s ﷻ way of saying: don’t worry, their time is coming. Every issue will be resolved, every injustice addressed, every lie exposed. There’s a saying, “Every dog has his day,” and Allah ﷻ is reminding the believers that for now, let them do what they’re doing. The Day of Settlement is coming.

Ayah 4

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَنۢبَآءِ مَا فِيهِ مُزْدَجَرٌ

And there has already come to them of information that in which there is deterrence.

The revelation they’ve received contains مُزْدَجَرٌ – strong scolding, powerful deterrence. Allah ﷻ acknowledges that parts of the Quran are meant to instill fear, to warn, to rebuke. This isn’t a weakness in the message; it’s part of its completeness. The Quran appeals to different aspects of the human being. Some passages speak to our intellect, others to our hearts, some instill hope, and others, like these, are meant to frighten us into taking action.

Ayah 5

حِكْمَةٌۢ بَـٰلِغَةٌ ۖ فَمَا تُغْنِ ٱلنُّذُرُ

[This is] mature wisdom, but warnings do not avail [them].

There is حِكْمَةٌۢ بَـٰلِغَةٌ – far-reaching wisdom, profound wisdom – even in the scolding portions of the Quran. Some might say, “Why does Allah ﷻ need to use fear? Why not just appeal to our intelligence?” But Allah ﷻ, the Creator of human beings, knows that we’re complex creatures. We need different approaches at different times. Sometimes we need intellectual arguments, sometimes emotional appeals, sometimes stern warnings.

Yet despite all this wisdom, despite the perfect balance in how the message is delivered, فَمَا تُغْنِ ٱلنُّذُرُ – the warnings don’t benefit them. Not because the warnings are inadequate, but because their hearts are closed.

Ayah 6

فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ۘ يَوْمَ يَدْعُ ٱلدَّاعِ إِلَىٰ شَىْءٍ نُّكُرٍ

So turn away from them. The Day the caller calls to something forbidding.

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ: فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ – turn away from them. If they refuse to listen, leave them. Your job is to deliver the message, not to force acceptance.

Then comes a chilling description of the Day of Judgment: يَوْمَ يَدْعُ ٱلدَّاعِ إِلَىٰ شَىْءٍ نُّكُرٍ – the Day when the caller will call to something unknown, something utterly alien. The trumpet will be blown, and people will hear a sound unlike anything they’ve ever experienced. The word nukur indicates something so strange, so unprecedented, that everyone’s first reaction will be: “What is that? I’ve never heard anything like that before.”

Ayah 7

خُشَّعًا أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرَادٌ مُّنتَشِرٌ

Their eyes humbled, they will emerge from the graves as if they were locusts spreading.

The scene intensifies: خُشَّعًا أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ – their eyes will be filled with horror and exhaustion. They’re emerging from ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ – graves that aren’t even marked anymore, graves that have been forgotten, that no one knew existed.

The image Allah ﷻ paints is vivid: كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرَادٌ مُّنتَشِرٌ – like locusts spreading everywhere. Have you ever seen a locust infestation? The insects pour out from everywhere, spreading in all directions. Or think of ants emerging from their colony – they just keep coming and coming, spreading out in waves. That’s what resurrection will look like: billions of human beings pouring out of the earth, spreading across the plains of judgment.

Ayah 8

مُّهْطِعِينَ إِلَى ٱلدَّاعِ ۖ يَقُولُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَسِرٌ

Racing ahead toward the caller. The disbelievers will say, “This is a difficult day.”

They will be مُّهْطِعِينَ – racing, running with their eyes fixed on a target. This word carries a specific meaning: when there’s chaos and fire everywhere, but you spot one place that seems stable, and you run toward it with your eyes locked on that target. That’s muhti’in. People will be desperate to reach the gathering place at Arafat on the Day of Judgment.

And the disbelievers will cry out: هَـٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَسِرٌ – this is an extremely difficult day. The word ’asir means something that squeezes you, that presses down on you with unbearable difficulty. This will be the day they finally realize the gravity of their choices.

Ayah 9

۞ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ فَكَذَّبُوا۟ عَبْدَنَا وَقَالُوا۟ مَجْنُونٌ وَٱزْدُجِرَ

The people of Nuh denied before them, and they denied Our servant and said, “A madman,” and he was repelled.

Now begins a series of historical accounts meant to comfort the Prophet ﷺ and warn his people. Before the Quraysh rejected Muhammad ﷺ, the people of Nuh (AS) did the same to their messenger. They called him مَجْنُونٌ – insane – and وَٱزْدُجِرَ – he was scolded and rebuked by them.

The irony is powerful. Earlier, Allah ﷻ said the revelation contains scolding (muzdajar). But here, it was the messenger himself who was scolded by his people. They inverted reality, treating the warner as if he were the one who needed correction.

Ayah 10

فَدَعَا رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنِّى مَغْلُوبٌ فَٱنتَصِرْ

So he invoked his Lord, “Indeed, I am overpowered, so help.”

After centuries of calling his people to truth, Nuh (AS) finally turned to Allah ﷻ: أَنِّى مَغْلُوبٌ – I am overpowered, I am overcome. Then he says فَٱنتَصِرْ – so avenge, so take revenge.

Notice something profound here: he doesn’t say “help me.” He says “avenge.” Why? Because Nuh (AS) is communicating that the offense isn’t against him personally. The offense is against Allah ﷻ. When people reject the messenger, they’re really rejecting the One who sent him. So Nuh (AS) is saying: You have the right to take revenge for Your message being rejected. This isn’t about me; this is about Your truth being denied.

Ayah 11

فَفَتَحْنَآ أَبْوَٰبَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَآءٍ مُّنْهَمِرٍ

So We opened the gates of the heaven with rain pouring down.

Allah ﷻ responds immediately: فَفَتَحْنَآ أَبْوَٰبَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ – We opened the gates of the sky. This teaches us something remarkable: the sky has doors, and they’re normally closed. When Allah ﷻ wills, He opens them.

The rain that came was بِمَآءٍ مُّنْهَمِرٍ – water that kept pouring down relentlessly. The word munhamir comes from the root meaning the first drop of rain, but here it indicates an intense downpour that wouldn’t stop. It just kept coming and coming, flooding the earth from above.

Ayah 12

وَفَجَّرْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا فَٱلْتَقَى ٱلْمَآءُ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ

And caused the earth to burst with springs, and the waters met for a matter already predestined.

But the flood didn’t come from just one direction. وَفَجَّرْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا – We made the earth burst forth with springs. The mountains around them began cracking open, and waterfalls started gushing out. It’s like filling a bathtub with both the shower and the faucet running – the water fills from above and below simultaneously.

فَٱلْتَقَى ٱلْمَآءُ – then the two waters met. The rain from the sky and the springs from the earth converged عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ – upon a matter that had already been decreed. This wasn’t chaos; this was a precisely executed divine plan.

Ayah 13

وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْوَٰحٍ وَدُسُرٍ

And We carried him on a [construction of] planks and nails.

In the midst of this catastrophic flood, Allah ﷻ describes the ark: وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْوَٰحٍ وَدُسُرٍ – We carried him on something made of planks and nails. Allah ﷻ had already told us that He taught Nuh (AS) how to build the ark. Now He reveals the basic engineering: boards (alwah) and nails (dusur).

This simple description emphasizes both the miracle and the practicality. The ark was a real, physical vessel made of wood and metal, yet it survived the greatest storm in human history. Some scholars believe the remains discovered in Turkey match this description, though certainty remains with Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 14

تَجْرِى بِأَعْيُنِنَا جَزَآءً لِّمَن كَانَ كُفِرَ

Sailing under Our observation as reward for he who had been denied.

The ark تَجْرِى بِأَعْيُنِنَا – sailed under Our watch, under Our eyes. Nuh (AS) had no experience navigating a ship, let alone in the worst storm ever to hit the earth. So who was the captain? Allah ﷻ Himself. The ship sailed under divine supervision.

This was جَزَآءً لِّمَن كَانَ كُفِرَ – the compensation for the one who was disbelieved in. Nuh (AS) had been rejected, mocked and denied for centuries. This was his vindication, his reward for patience and perseverance.

Ayah 15

وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَـٰهَآ ءَايَةً فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ

And We left it as a sign, so is there any who will remember?

Allah ﷻ says وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَـٰهَآ ءَايَةً – We have left it as a sign, as a miracle. The ark would be discovered, would be found by future generations as evidence of what happened.

Then comes the question: فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ – is there anyone who will remember? This question deserves careful attention because of the word muddakir. In Arabic, you have dhikr (remembrance), then dhakir (one who remembers), then mutadakkir (one who puts effort into remembering). The letters ta and dal together are difficult to pronounce: mutadakkir. So the word can be made easier: muddakkir (merging the letters into two dals). Even easier: muddakir (the easiest pronunciation).

When a word is made easier to pronounce, it rhetorically communicates that less effort is required in the action itself. Allah ﷻ uses the easiest form – muddakir – to say: I’m not asking for someone who exerts tremendous effort to remember. I’m not even asking for moderate effort. Is there anyone out there who will put even the smallest bit of effort into taking this reminder?

This question will repeat throughout the surah, especially after each story of destruction. It’s a call to us: will you put in even minimal effort to learn this Book, to understand it, to memorize its vocabulary, to take its guidance? Allah ﷻ has made the Quran easy, so the effort required from us is minimal compared to the benefit we’ll receive.

Ayah 16

فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ

So how were My punishment and warning?

This question also repeats throughout the surah: فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ – How were My punishment and My warnings? Allah ﷻ is asking: Was that enough for you? Did you see what happened when people persisted in rejection? The question hangs in the air, demanding reflection.

Ayah 17

وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ

And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?

This ayah will also repeat, forming a refrain throughout the surah. وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ – We have absolutely made the Quran easy for remembrance. The word yassarna emphasizes that Allah ﷻ has removed obstacles, has made it accessible.

The word dhikr here carries multiple meanings: remembrance of Allah ﷻ, taking heed of warnings, memorization, understanding, and acting upon guidance. The Quran has been made easy for all these purposes. After mentioning destroyed nations, Allah ﷻ asks: will you take the lesson? Will you put in the effort to learn and benefit from this Book?

Ayah 18

كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ

’Aad denied, so how were My punishment and warning?

Now comes the second historical account: كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ denied the truth. This was the nation of the Prophet Hud (AS), known for their physical strength and architectural achievements. Despite all their power, they rejected their messenger.

Ayah 19

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِى يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُّسْتَمِرٍّ

Indeed, We sent upon them a screaming wind on a day of continuous misfortune.

Their punishment came as رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا – an extremely cold, harsh and relentless wind, a freezing wind that wouldn’t stop. It came فِى يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُّسْتَمِرٍّ – on a day of continuous curse, a day of relentless misfortune. The word mustamirr (continuous, unceasing) appears again, just as it did when describing the magic the disbelievers accused the Prophet ﷺ of performing. But here it describes the punishment that wouldn’t stop.

Ayah 20

تَنزِعُ ٱلنَّاسَ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ مُّنقَعِرٍ

Extracting the people as if they were trunks of palm trees uprooted.

The wind تَنزِعُ ٱلنَّاسَ – plucked people up. The word nazi’ is used when you pull a plant out of the ground by its roots and discard it. People were being lifted and thrown around by this wind كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ مُّنقَعِرٍ – as if they were the uprooted trunks of palm trees that were hollowed out.

Picture dead palm tree roots that have been eaten away by termites on the inside, leaving them hollow and easy to carry. That’s what these people looked like – their bodies were there, but they were destroyed from within. Their bones were all broken, leaving them like hollow sticks being tossed around by the wind. This is the image Allah ﷻ paints of the mighty nation of ’Aad in their final moments.

Ayah 21

فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ

So how were My punishment and warning?

Again the question: How were My punishment and My warnings? Each story builds upon the last, each asking us the same question: Are you paying attention? Are you taking heed?

Ayah 22

وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ

And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?

And again: We’ve made the Quran easy for you to take guidance from. Is there anyone out there putting in even a little effort?

Ayah 23

كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِٱلنُّذُرِ

Thamud denied the warnings.

The third nation: Thamud, the people of the Prophet Salih (AS). They rejected the warnings that came to them.

Ayah 24

فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَبَشَرًا مِّنَّا وَٰحِدًا نَّتَّبِعُهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ وَسُعُرٍ

And said, “Is it a human being from among us – one alone – we are to follow? Indeed, we would then be in error and madness.”

Their objection was the same as others before them: أَبَشَرًا مِّنَّا وَٰحِدًا نَّتَّبِعُهُۥٓ – Is it just a human being from among us, just one guy, that we’re supposed to follow? This arrogance – the refusal to accept that a messenger could be one of them – has been the downfall of nations throughout history.

They continued: إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ وَسُعُرٍ – if we did that, we would be in clear misguidance and madness. The word su’ur refers to the flames of insanity, being crazy. They’re saying: we’d have to be completely out of our minds to follow him.

Ayah 25

أَءُلْقِىَ ٱلذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنۢ بَيْنِنَا بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ

Has the message been sent down upon him from among us? Rather, he is an insolent liar.

They continued their mockery: أَءُلْقِىَ ٱلذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنۢ بَيْنِنَا – Has the reminder been cast upon him from among all of us? Out of everyone here, he’s the one God chose? There was nobody else?

بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ – Rather, he is a relentless liar and extremely arrogant and wild. The word ashir describes someone who is unstable, wild, uncontrollable in their arrogance. This was their final verdict on Prophet Salih (AS).

Ayah 26

سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَدًا مَّنِ ٱلْكَذَّابُ ٱلْأَشِرُ

They will know tomorrow who is the insolent liar.

Allah ﷻ responds – or perhaps He informed Prophet Salih (AS) to tell them: سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَدًا مَّنِ ٱلْكَذَّابُ ٱلْأَشِرُ – Tomorrow you will find out who the liar is, and who is wild and arrogant. The tables will turn. They accused him of these qualities, but they’ll discover these qualities were in themselves.

Ayah 27

إِنَّا مُرْسِلُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَّهُمْ فَٱرْتَقِبْهُمْ وَٱصْطَبِرْ

Indeed, We are sending the she-camel as a trial for them, so watch them and be patient.

Allah ﷻ sent them a miraculous she-camel as a test: إِنَّا مُرْسِلُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَّهُمْ – We are sending the she-camel as a trial for them. Then He commands Salih (AS): فَٱرْتَقِبْهُمْ – keep close watch on them, وَٱصْطَبِرْ – and maintain your patience. Be extremely patient with them.

The camel that Allah ﷻ sent was so enormous that when it came to drink at their water source, all the other animals would scatter in fear. The people complained: we can’t get our animals to drink when this camel is around.

Ayah 28

وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ ٱلْمَآءَ قِسْمَةٌۢ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُّحْتَضَرٌ

And inform them that the water is shared between them, each [day of] drink attended [by turn].

So the solution was revealed: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ ٱلْمَآءَ قِسْمَةٌۢ بَيْنَهُمْ – inform them that the water is to be divided between them. One day for the camel, one day for their animals.

كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُّحْتَضَرٌ – each drinking place would be attended by its designated party. The word muhtadar means one who attends something while the owner is not around. So Salih (AS) didn’t even have to stay and watch the camel; they had agreed to the arrangement. One day was for the camel, one day for them. It was understood, and they had accepted these terms.

Ayah 29

فَنَادَوْا۟ صَاحِبَهُمْ فَتَعَاطَىٰ فَعَقَرَ

But they called their companion, and he dared and hamstrung [her].

Despite the agreement, فَنَادَوْا۟ صَاحِبَهُمْ – they called upon one of their guys, one of their companions. This was the most wicked among them, a man of violence. فَتَعَاطَىٰ – he drew out his sword, and فَعَقَرَ – he hamstrung the camel, stabbing it in the neck.

The word ’aqr specifically refers to striking the neck. Since the camel was so tall, its neck was exposed and vulnerable. This criminal took his sword and stabbed the miraculous she-camel in the neck, killing it. This is the same person mentioned later in the Quran in Surah Ash-Shams, one of the most evil individuals in history.

They got one of their “tough guys” to do the job – the kind of person you call when you have a problem you want handled through violence. He took care of it, as they say, and murdered the she-camel that Allah ﷻ had sent as a sign.

Ayah 30

فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ

So how were My punishment and warning?

Once again: How were My punishment and My warnings?

Ayah 31

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَكَانُوا۟ كَهَشِيمِ ٱلْمُحْتَظِرِ

Indeed, We sent upon them one blast from the sky, and they became like the dry twig fragments of an [animal] pen.

Their punishment was swift: إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً – We sent against them a single loud scream, one blast from the sky.

فَكَانُوا۟ كَهَشِيمِ ٱلْمُحْتَظِرِ – then they became like the dry twig fragments of an animal pen. When you build a fence for livestock using branches and twigs, small pieces break off and fall to the ground – dry, brittle leftovers from construction. That’s what these people looked like after the blast: rubble, debris, broken fragments scattered on the ground.

Ayah 32

وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ

And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?

The refrain returns: We’ve made the Quran easy for remembrance. Is there anyone out there who will put in even minimal effort to learn from these lessons?

Ayah 33

كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍۭ بِٱلنُّذُرِ

The people of Lut denied the warnings.

Now comes the fourth account: كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍۭ بِٱلنُّذُرِ – the people of Lut denied the warnings. This was a nation that had sunk into a particular form of moral corruption that Prophet Lut (AS) was sent to address.

Ayah 34

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ ۖ نَّجَّيْنَـٰهُم بِسَحَرٍ

Indeed, We sent upon them a storm of stones, except the family of Lut – We saved them before dawn.

Their punishment was unique: إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا – We sent against them a fierce storm of stones. But there was an exception: إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ – except the family of Lut.

نَّجَّيْنَـٰهُم بِسَحَرٍ – We saved them in the early hours before dawn. The word sahar refers to the last part of the night, just before Fajr, when darkness is at its deepest but dawn is approaching. This was when Allah ﷻ extracted the believers from among a doomed nation, saving them before the punishment fell.

These stories aren’t just history lessons; they’re mirrors held up to every generation. The patterns repeat: arrogance, rejection of truth, following desires, persecution of messengers, and ultimately, divine justice. And after each story, Allah ﷻ asks: Have We not made the Quran easy for you to learn from? Is there anyone out there who will take these lessons to heart?

Ayah 34–55 · linked video: 02. Al-Qamar (Ayah 34-55)

Surah Al-Qamar: Commentary on Ayahs 34-55

Ayah 34

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ ۖ نَّجَّيْنَـٰهُم بِسَحَرٍ

We saved them by the morning time. Allah ﷻ rescued the family of Prophet Lut (AS) at the time of dawn, just before the punishment descended upon his people. This rescue of the Prophet (AS) and his believing followers was itself a mercy and a reward from Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 35

نِّعْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى مَن شَكَرَ

Allah ﷻ says: “This was a great favor that came especially from Us.” The word نِّعْمَةً emphasizes that their salvation was a divine gift. Then Allah ﷻ establishes a principle: كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى مَن شَكَرَ - “That is how We compensate the one who is grateful.”

Just as Allah ﷻ previously compensated and rewarded Prophet Nuh (AS) by providing him with the ship for salvation, the rescue of the prophets is considered one of their rewards. The rescue itself becomes part of the recompense for those who show gratitude to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 36

وَلَقَدْ أَنذَرَهُم بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا۟ بِٱلنُّذُرِ

Prophet Lut (AS) had warned them of Allah’s punishment, but they remained in doubt about it. The word تَمَارَوْا۟ indicates that they weren’t just uncertain, they actively discussed their doubts with one another. They conversed among themselves about whether the warnings were true, dismissing what the Prophet (AS) had clearly conveyed to them.

Ayah 37

وَلَقَدْ رَٰوَدُوهُ عَن ضَيْفِهِۦ فَطَمَسْنَآ أَعْيُنَهُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ

They were intensely interested in and inclined toward his guests. These guests were the angels who had come in the form of young, handsome men to this town overwhelmed by homosexuals and criminals. When they saw these visitors, they ran toward the house of Lut (AS) and tried to take advantage of those angels, not knowing who they were truly dealing with.

Allah ﷻ declares: فَطَمَسْنَآ أَعْيُنَهُمْ - “We blinded their eyes.” The word طَمَسَ means to rub out, to obliterate. There’s an interesting parallel here between the Quranic and biblical accounts. The biblical version describes one of the angels passing his arm in front of them, causing all the people to be blinded in one strike. The Quran summarizes this event and attributes the action directly to Allah ﷻ: “We crushed their eyes.”

Then comes the command: فَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ - “Taste My punishment and My warnings.” Notice the shift in address. In all the previous nations discussed in this surah, Allah ﷻ asked: “How was My punishment?” - directing the question to the people of Makkah as observers. But here, Allah’s anger is so intense regarding the people of Lut (AS) that He addresses them directly, not even the Makkans. The subject changes to confront the criminals themselves: “You people, taste My punishment and My warnings.”

Ayah 38

وَلَقَدْ صَبَّحَهُم بُكْرَةً عَذَابٌ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ

By the morning time, what came to them? The verb صَبَّحَ specifically means something that comes to you in the morning. What came: عَذَابٌ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ - a punishment that would stay, a punishment that took permanent hold. This wasn’t a temporary affliction but an established, enduring torment.

Ayah 39

فَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ

Once again: “Taste My punishment and My warnings.” The repetition emphasizes the severity and finality of what befell them.

Ayah 40

وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ

And We have made the Quran easy for remembrance. This refrain appears throughout the surah, reminding us that these stories, these lessons, these warnings are all preserved in a Book that Allah ﷻ has made accessible. The question follows: فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ - “Is there anyone who will take heed? Anyone who will make an effort to remember?”

Ayah 41

وَلَقَدْ جَآءَ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ ٱلنُّذُرُ

The surah now shifts to another nation: the people of Pharaoh. The warnings came to them as well. The term ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ refers to Pharaoh’s lineage, his descendants, his people, his entire system.

Ayah 42

كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا كُلِّهَا فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ

They denied all Our signs. They were given nine miraculous signs through Prophet Musa (AS), yet they rejected every single one. So Allah ﷻ declares: فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ - “We seized them with the seizing of One who is Mighty and Omnipotent.”

Why does Allah ﷻ mention both عَزِيز (the Mighty) and مُّقْتَدِر (the Omnipotent)? Because Pharaoh claimed authority unlike any other nation mentioned in this surah. The others had arrogance, but nothing compared to Pharaoh’s claims. He declared himself divine, claiming ultimate authority. So he had to be grabbed by the One with true authority. Pharaoh thought he possessed tremendous power, so Allah ﷻ doesn’t just mention His might, but also His absolute control - Muqtadir, the One who controls all power.

Ayah 43

أَكُفَّارُكُمْ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكُمْ أَمْ لَكُم بَرَآءَةٌ فِى ٱلزُّبُرِ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the disbelievers of Makkah directly: “Are your disbelievers better than those people We already destroyed?” Do you think you’re any better than the nations of Nuh, Hud, Salih, Lut, and Musa (peace be upon them all)?

أَمْ لَكُم بَرَآءَةٌ فِى ٱلزُّبُرِ - “Or do you have some kind of immunity that came to you in the scriptures?” Do you possess some escape letter, some divine pass that exempts you from accountability? Do you think you’re going to be the exception?

Ayah 44

أَمْ يَقُولُونَ نَحْنُ جَمِيعٌ مُّنتَصِرٌ

Or are these people saying: “We are a strong gathering that cannot be dominated”? The word مُّنتَصِر means those who take revenge, who are never defeated. They’re claiming: “We’re not the ones who get dominated. Revenge isn’t taken from us - we take it from others. We’re invincible together.”

Ayah 45

سَيُهْزَمُ ٱلْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ ٱلدُّبُرَ

Allah ﷻ responds with a prophecy: “The entire gathering will be defeated.” The word سَيُهْزَمُ indicates a future event. Your entire might is going to be done away with. وَيُوَلُّونَ ٱلدُّبُرَ - “And they will turn their backs” - meaning they’ll flee in retreat.

This is Allah ﷻ alluding to what would happen at the Battle of Badr. He’s essentially saying: “You think you’re so strong? You think you’ll take revenge? Let’s see what happens at Badr.” The entire gathering of the Quraysh would be routed, and they would show their backs fleeing from the Muslims.

This was revealed 15 years before the Battle of Badr. When the battle actually occurred, it looked very unlikely that the Muslims would succeed. The situation was so dire that the Prophet ﷺ made an extended supplication to Allah ﷻ, saying: “These are 15 years of my labor. Don’t let them go to waste today. If they are killed today, then nobody will be saying Your name after this.” He kept making this supplication - one of the longest recorded - until Allah ﷻ revealed that his prayer had been answered and that he should stop. Some scholars say this is actually the reason the Quraysh even survived that day - because the Prophet ﷺ stopped supplicating against them. Had he not stopped, they all would have been annihilated at Badr.

Ayah 46

بَلِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ مَوْعِدُهُمْ وَٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْهَىٰ وَأَمَرُّ

However, the real appointment for them is the Hour. Badr is just a preview. The actual meeting, the real reckoning, will be on the Day of Judgment. وَٱلسَّاعَةُ أَدْهَىٰ وَأَمَرُّ - “And the Hour is a greater catastrophe and more bitter.”

The word أَدْهَىٰ means a tremendous calamity, a disaster. And أَمَرُّ means more bitter, more sour-tasting. Losing a battle leaves a bitter taste in your mouth, but that’s nothing compared to Judgment Day. The bitterness of that Day is incomparable.

Ayah 47

إِنَّ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ وَسُعُرٍ

These criminals are the ones truly in misguidance, and they are the ones who are insane. Earlier, the disbelievers said about the Prophet ﷺ: “How can we follow this guy? If we follow him, we’d have to be crazy. We’d have to be really lost and insane.” Allah ﷻ turns this back on them: No, you are the lost ones. You are the insane ones. You’re crazy not to follow him.

Ayah 48

يَوْمَ يُسْحَبُونَ فِى ٱلنَّارِ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ ذُوقُوا۟ مَسَّ سَقَرَ

On the Day when they will be dragged into the Fire on their faces. The word يُسْحَبُونَ means to be held down, restrained. Imagine someone trying to get up and being forced back down repeatedly - like a criminal under arrest trying to stand and the officer pushing them back down. Or picture a cage so low you can’t even stand, so you’re constantly held in that position.

They’ll be dragged عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ - on their faces. Then they’ll be told: ذُوقُوا۟ مَسَّ سَقَرَ - “Taste the touch of the raging flame,” taste what it feels like to be touched by Saqar, one of the names of Hellfire.

Ayah 49

إِنَّا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقْنَـٰهُ بِقَدَرٍ

Indeed, everything We have created, We created with precise measure. Everything is measured: lives are measured, the lifespan of Makkah is measured, the rule of the Quraysh is measured, how long one civilization will live, how long one individual will live - all of it is precisely calculated and kept track of by Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 50

وَمَآ أَمْرُنَآ إِلَّا وَٰحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍۭ بِٱلْبَصَرِ

And Our command is nothing but a single word, like the blink of an eye. This ayah addresses a profound reality: when Allah ﷻ issues a command, its execution is instantaneous. The closest thing we can use to comprehend how His commands are carried out is the blink of an eye, a moment of a glance.

For us, destroying an army, obliterating a town, or bringing a flood that covers the entire earth takes extensive planning, tremendous work, and the gathering of countless resources. But for Allah ﷻ? It’s the blink of an eye. That’s how easy it is for Him to end any struggle, to change any situation completely.

Ayah 51

وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَآ أَشْيَاعَكُمْ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ

We have already destroyed many of your kind - أَشْيَاعَكُمْ , meaning your types, your brand of disbelievers. Many like you have already been taken out. فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ - “Is there anyone who will take heed?”

Throughout this surah, Allah ﷻ keeps emphasizing that the Quran is easy to remember, easy to take warning from. He says: take remembrance, take the warning. And now He asks: “Your kind has already been destroyed. Is that even enough for you to hear? Is that sufficient for you to take the warning seriously?”

Ayah 52

وَكُلُّ شَىْءٍ فَعَلُوهُ فِى ٱلزُّبُرِ

Everything they have done is already recorded in previous scriptures. Their crimes are on record. You can compare their crimes to yours and find countless parallels. The patterns of disbelief, the arrogance, the rejection of truth - it’s all been documented before.

Ayah 53

وَكُلُّ صَغِيرٍ وَكَبِيرٍ مُّسْتَطَرٌ

And speaking of your crimes: every small and large thing is مُّسْتَطَرٌ - written down, documented line by line. There’s a detailed, line-item record of everything you’re doing. Nothing escapes documentation. Every deed, whether minor or major, is preserved in the divine record.

Ayah 54

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَنَهَرٍ

After all these warnings about punishment and destruction, Allah ﷻ shifts to the reward of the believers. Indeed, the people of Taqwa - those who protected themselves, who were conscious of Allah ﷻ - will be in gardens.

Then Allah ﷻ adds: وَنَهَرٍ - and there will be a special river. Usually Allah ﷻ says جَنَّـٰتٍ وَأَنْهَارٍ - gardens and rivers (plural). But here, He mentions a singular, special river. They’re going to be فِى نَهَرٍ - actually in the river, swimming in it. This is a unique river made especially for the people of Paradise, distinct from the regular rivers of Jannah.

Ayah 55

فِى مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِندَ مَلِيكٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍۭ

They will be فِى مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ - in a seat of truth, a place of true seating. The word مَقْعَد , as opposed to مَجْلِس , indicates a place where you sit for a long time, where you don’t want to get up.

But this raises a question: why would anyone want to sit in one place in Paradise? There’s so much to see, so many places to explore. Why would you want to remain seated?

The answer lies in what makes this seating so special: عِندَ مَلِيكٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍۭ - it’s in the company of the King, the eternal King, the One who controls all things. When Allah ﷻ offers you a seat close to Him in Paradise, why would you want to move? You might grow tired of anyone’s company in this world, but that’s not company you’ll ever tire of.

This is the seating of truth - truly, this is the place to sit. In Surah Ar-Rahman, Allah ﷻ also describes the believers reclining on beautiful couches with exquisite decorations surrounding them. And here at the end of Surah Al-Qamar, He mentions this wonderful seating once again. This is the seating we should all be striving for, the place we’re all working toward.

When the Companions (may Allah be pleased with them) heard these descriptions, they were in the middle of tremendous struggle. They had given up comfort, the love of family, economic security - everything had become difficult because they had joined ranks with Muhammad ﷺ. The only thing keeping their hopes alive was these very descriptions: “I want to sit on that seat. I want to swim in that river. I want to be in those gardens.”

These passages were what kept them motivated constantly. The Companions used to recite the Quran all the time, and there’s a profound reason for this: when you read so much about the unseen, it starts becoming visible to you. When you engage deeply with the Quran, your entire worldview transforms. You don’t see grass the way you looked at it before. You don’t see the sun the way you looked at it before. You don’t see laughter and tears the way you perceived them previously.

The purpose of Makki Quran is precisely this: to change our perspective on life, on everything around us. Everything becomes an ayah - a sign pointing to Allah ﷻ. This is the gift these passages offer us, and this is why we must build our own relationship with them.

The surah began by reminding us of the miracle of the moon splitting, which people refused to accept. But by the end, the tone has shifted. Though most of the surah consists of warnings, Allah ﷻ doesn’t want to conclude with the negative. So He ends with hope, with the positive. He doesn’t simply say the people of Taqwa won’t burn or won’t be destroyed. No - He tells us they’ll have gardens, a wonderful river, and a seat in a very high place, in the company of the King Himself.

This is the balance of the Quran: stern warnings followed by beautiful hope, constantly reminding us that the path to that blessed seating is open to anyone who chooses to walk it.
